content:
The **perceptron** was introduced by **Frank Rosenblatt** at the **Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory** in **1957–1958**, inspired by early brain models and **Warren McCulloch & Walter Pitts**'s **1943** formal neuron (weighted inputs + threshold logic).

Rosenblatt's **Mark I Perceptron** (1958) was a hardware demonstrator for visual pattern recognition — a single-layer system that captured public imagination during the first AI wave.

**Marvin Minsky** (Rosenblatt's Cornell colleague) later co-authored **"Perceptrons"** (1969) with **Seymour Papert**, proving limitations of **single-layer** linear threshold networks — notably that a single perceptron cannot represent **XOR**. That result contributed to reduced funding for neural nets until **multilayer** models and **backpropagation** revived the field in the **1980s**.

The **Perceptron Convergence Theorem** (Novikoff, 1962; others) showed that if data is **linearly separable**, the Rosenblatt rule finds a separating hyperplane in finite steps — grounding the **linear separability** story taught in this topic.

Modern deep learning stacks many units with **smooth activations**, but every MLP still begins with the same **weighted sum + bias** pattern defined here.

timeline.label: McCulloch–Pitts neuron (1943)
timeline.description: Mathematical model of binary threshold units — precursor to weighted sum + step activation.

timeline.label: Rosenblatt perceptron (1957–58)
timeline.description: Learning rule adjusting weights on errors; Mark I hardware demo for pattern recognition.

timeline.label: Convergence theorem (1962)
timeline.description: Proof that separable data yields finite-step convergence of the perceptron algorithm.

timeline.label: Minsky & Papert Perceptrons (1969)
timeline.description: Formal linear separability limits (XOR); motivates multilayer networks and later deep learning revival.
